title: Becoming the Teacher
subtitle: Capstone, the Road Ahead, and How to Teach This Course
theme: Synthesis, honesty about the unknown, and the handing-over of the torch — from student to teacher.
learningObjectives: Retrace the full arc of the course — from attention to agents — as one coherent story you can tell from memory.
Articulate the genuinely open problems in AI as of mid-2026: reasoning limits, alignment, cost, and data walls.
Reason critically about where AI may go next — AGI debates, world models, embodied AI — without hype or doom.
Translate the journey into concrete career and business decisions for the next 1–3 years.
Teach this material to a live audience: pace it, demo it, field questions, and correct the common misconceptions.
Leave with a personal capstone and a credible plan to pass the knowledge on.
storyHook: Twenty days ago we started with eight words from a 2017 paper: 'Attention is all you need.' We have travelled from that single equation to autonomous agents that use your tools, see your screen, and do your work. Today we reach the summit, look back at the whole climb, and then do the bravest thing a learner can do — turn around, face the people coming up behind us, and teach. By the end of this session you are no longer a student of this revolution. You are one of its guides.

slides.title: Welcome to the Summit
slides.body:
## You made it

Take a breath. Nineteen days ago, terms like *self-attention*, *RLHF*, *mixture-of-experts*, *MCP*, and *agentic orchestration* may have been fog. Today they are tools in your hands. That transformation — fog to fluency — is the whole point of this course.

Today is different from the other nineteen. It has three movements:

1. **Look back** — we retell the entire 20-day story as a single, gripping narrative, so it lives in you as *one idea*, not twenty.
2. **Look out** — we face what we still don't know: the open problems, the debates, the plausible futures. Honesty is part of mastery.
3. **Look forward — and turn around** — we translate it all into your career and your business, and then I teach you how to *teach this*. Because knowledge you can't pass on is knowledge half-owned.

## The promise of today

You will leave with three things: a story you can tell, a clear-eyed map of the unknown, and the confidence to stand in front of a room and lead others up the same climb. The torch is being handed over. Let's make sure you can carry it.
slides.speakerNotes: Set a celebratory, slightly emotional tone — these people finished a marathon. Ask: 'Think back to Day 1. What's one term you now understand that was a complete mystery then?' Let a few people share; it makes the growth tangible and bonds the room before the heavy synthesis work.
slides.marketConnection: As of mid-2026, people who can both *understand* and *explain* modern AI are scarcer and more valuable than those who can do only one — this day is about becoming both.

slides.title: The Whole Story in One Breath
slides.body:
## The 20-day arc as a single narrative

Here is the entire journey, told as one story. Practice telling it; it's the backbone of everything you'll teach.

> In 2017, researchers asked: what if we drop recurrence and let every word look at every other word at once? **Attention** was all you need — and the **Transformer** was born. Scale it up on oceans of text and a strange thing happens: it doesn't just complete sentences, it picks up *capabilities* nobody programmed in — **emergence**. We learned to **pre-train** a giant base model, then **fine-tune** and **align** it with human feedback (**RLHF**) so it became helpful and safe instead of merely fluent. We taught it to **reason** by thinking step by step, then by spending real compute to deliberate. We gave it more than text — images, audio, video — making it **multimodal**. We grounded it in facts with **retrieval (RAG)** so it could cite truth instead of hallucinate. We shrank and sped it up with **quantization, distillation, and mixture-of-experts** so it could run cheaply, even on your laptop. And finally, we put it in a **loop**, handed it **tools** through a universal standard (**MCP**), gave it **eyes and a mouse** (computer use), and let it **act** — the **agent**. Eight years; one straight line from a single attention head to an autonomous worker.

## The five eras

```
  2017-2019  THE ARCHITECTURE   Transformers, attention, scale
  2020-2022  THE SCALE-UP       big LMs, emergence, few-shot
  2022-2023  THE ALIGNMENT      RLHF, ChatGPT, the public moment
  2023-2024  THE SENSES+TRUTH   multimodal, RAG, reasoning, efficiency
  2025-2026  THE AGENTS         tools, MCP, computer use, autonomy
```

Every topic you studied is a station on this one line. If a learner ever feels lost, bring them back to this story — it's the map that makes the details make sense.
slides.speakerNotes: Read the long quote aloud, slowly, like a story by a campfire — this is the single most teachable artifact in the whole course. Then ask the class to try retelling the arc to a neighbor in 60 seconds. Whoever can tell this story owns the material. The five-era table is the cheat sheet for that story.
slides.marketConnection: Being able to compress eight years of AI into one coherent 90-second story is exactly the skill that makes you credible to executives and newcomers alike in the 2026 market.

slides.title: What We Got Right — The Lessons That Held
slides.body:
## The durable truths of this revolution

Among all the churn, a few principles proved themselves again and again. These are the load-bearing ideas — teach them as bedrock.

- **Scale works — stunningly, predictably.** The scaling laws held far longer than skeptics believed. More data, more parameters, more compute reliably bought more capability. This was the central bet, and it paid off.
- **Generality beats specialization.** One big model fine-tuned for many tasks beat armies of bespoke narrow models. The 'foundation model' idea reshaped the entire field.
- **Alignment is a feature, not an afterthought.** The leap from GPT-3 to ChatGPT wasn't mostly raw capability — it was *alignment*. Making a model helpful and safe is what made it useful to humanity.
- **Capability without grounding hallucinates; grounding without capability is dumb.** RAG and tools taught us that intelligence must be *connected* to truth and to the world to be trustworthy.
- **The system beats the model.** As we saw on Day 19, the frontier matured from 'best model' to 'best system around the model.' Engineering, not just intelligence, ships value.

## Why these matter for teaching

When students get lost in jargon, these five truths are the anchors. Every technique in the course is a way of expressing one of them. Master the principles and the details become *consequences* rather than facts to memorize.
slides.speakerNotes: Frame these as 'the things that survived the hype cycle.' Ask the class which of the five surprised them most — for many it's that scale kept working. These principles are your fallback whenever a learner is drowning in detail; pull them back up to the principle.
slides.marketConnection: These principles explain why mid-2026 investment still flows toward scale, alignment, and systems engineering rather than toward exotic narrow architectures.

slides.title: The Open Problems: Where the Map Says 'Here Be Dragons'
slides.body:
## Mastery includes knowing the limits

A real expert is honest about what's unsolved. As of mid-2026, four great problems define the frontier — and anyone who tells you they're solved is selling something.

### 1. Reasoning limits
Models reason impressively yet still stumble in revealing ways: brittle on truly novel problems, prone to confident nonsense, and there's live debate over whether 'thinking' models genuinely reason or perform a very sophisticated pattern-match. Long-horizon reliability — staying coherent over hundreds of steps — remains the bottleneck for agents.

### 2. Alignment & control
We can make models *mostly* helpful and harmless, but we can't yet *guarantee* it. As models get more capable and more autonomous, ensuring they pursue what we actually want — and don't deceive, manipulate, or pursue misaligned goals — is unsolved, and the stakes rise with capability.

### 3. Cost & energy
Frontier training and large-scale inference burn enormous compute, money, and electricity. Efficiency improved dramatically, but so did demand. The economics and the energy/datacenter footprint are real constraints, not footnotes.

### 4. The data wall
We may be running low on high-quality human text to train on. The proposed escapes — synthetic data, learning from richer modalities, learning from interaction with the world — are promising but unproven at frontier scale. Whether the scaling party continues partly hinges on this.

## The honest stance

```
  SOLVED-ish        IN PROGRESS         OPEN / HARD
  fluency           multimodality       guaranteed alignment
  basic alignment   tool use            robust novel reasoning
  efficiency wins   agent reliability   the data wall
                                         cost/energy at scale
```
slides.speakerNotes: This slide builds trust with sophisticated audiences — it proves you're not a hype merchant. Pick the 'do they really reason?' debate and present *both* sides fairly; it models the intellectual honesty you want learners to adopt. Ask: 'Which of these four worries you most for your industry?' Great discussion starter.
slides.marketConnection: These four open problems are precisely where the largest mid-2026 research budgets, safety teams, and energy/datacenter investments are pointed.

slides.title: The AGI Question — Without the Hype or the Doom
slides.body:
## The most loaded three letters in tech

No course on modern AI can dodge **AGI** — Artificial General Intelligence, loosely 'AI that matches or exceeds humans across most cognitive work.' As of mid-2026 the debate is loud, sincere, and unresolved. Your job as a teacher is not to pick a side but to give learners a *framework* to think clearly.

## The three honest camps

- **The near-believers:** scaling plus agents plus reasoning is a straight road to AGI, possibly within years. Capabilities keep surprising us; extrapolate the curve.
- **The skeptics:** current models, however impressive, are missing something fundamental — true understanding, causal models, robust reasoning — and more scale won't conjure it. We've climbed a tall hill, not the mountain.
- **The 'wrong question' camp:** 'AGI' is too fuzzy to be useful. What matters is concrete capability on concrete tasks, deployed responsibly — call it what you like.

## How to hold this as a teacher

Resist both the breathless 'it's basically here' and the dismissive 'it's just autocomplete.' The defensible position: *the systems are genuinely, surprisingly capable and improving fast; they also have real, possibly fundamental limitations; and reasonable, well-informed experts disagree about what comes next.* That humility is not weakness — it's the most accurate map we have. Teach learners to update their beliefs as evidence arrives, not to anchor to a prophecy.
slides.speakerNotes: This is where rooms get heated — embrace it, but referee fairly. Present all three camps with genuine respect; if you visibly favor one, you lose half the room's trust. The line to deliver with conviction: 'Smart, informed people disagree — and being comfortable with that uncertainty is itself a sign of expertise.' Ask the room to self-sort into camps and defend their view briefly.
slides.marketConnection: The AGI debate directly shapes mid-2026 capital flows, regulation, and corporate strategy — leaders look to people who can discuss it soberly, neither hyping nor dismissing.

slides.title: What May Come Next: World Models and Embodiment
slides.body:
## Reading the road ahead

Nobody can predict AI precisely — we just watched a field reinvent itself twice. But several directions have real momentum as of mid-2026. Teach these as *plausible bets*, not prophecies.

### World models
Today's models learn mostly from text and images — a shadow of reality. **World models** aim to learn an internal, predictive model of how the physical and causal world *works* — to imagine consequences, plan, and reason about cause and effect rather than just correlate. Many researchers see this as a missing piece for robust reasoning and a route past the data wall (learn from simulation and interaction, not just scraped text).

### Embodied AI & robotics
If agents conquered the *digital* world in 2025–2026, the next frontier is the *physical* one. Pair a capable multimodal 'brain' with a robot body and you get machines that perceive, plan, and act in the real world. Progress in robotics learning accelerated sharply; humanoid and general-purpose robots moved from labs toward early deployment. The same agent loop you learned — think, act, observe — but the tools are *motors and sensors*.

### Other live threads
- **Continual / lifelong learning** — models that keep learning after deployment instead of being frozen.
- **Personal, persistent agents** — an AI that knows you, remembers across years, and acts on your behalf.
- **Scientific discovery** — AI as a research partner in biology, materials, and math.

## The throughline

```
  text  ->  images  ->  tools  ->  screens  ->  the physical world
  (the steady march from describing reality to acting within it)
```

Each era extended AI's reach further into reality. The next steps continue that same arrow.
slides.speakerNotes: Frame these as 'where to point your curiosity for the next two years,' not guarantees. The 'text to physical world' arrow is the unifying idea — it shows these aren't random predictions but the natural continuation of the course's whole arc. Ask: 'Which of these would change YOUR industry most?'
slides.marketConnection: World models, robotics/embodied AI, and persistent personal agents attracted major mid-2026 research and venture attention as the candidate 'next big platform' after digital agents.

slides.title: What This Means for Your Career
slides.body:
## The honest career conversation

Learners will ask, sometimes anxiously: *what does this mean for my job?* Give them clarity, not platitudes.

## The shift, in one line

**AI doesn't replace people who do tasks; it replaces tasks. The people who thrive move up the stack — from doing the task to directing and verifying the AI that does it.** The developer became a reviewer of agent code. The analyst became a director of research agents. The pattern repeats everywhere.

## The durable skills

| Rising in value | Falling in value |
|---|---|
| Judgment, taste, knowing what 'good' looks like | Rote execution that an agent can do |
| Asking the right questions / framing problems | Memorized procedural knowledge |
| Orchestrating and verifying AI output | Resistance to using AI at all |
| Domain depth + AI fluency together | Generic, undifferentiated output |
| Ethics, trust, and human relationships | — |

## Concrete moves for the next 1–3 years

1. **Become AI-fluent in your own domain** — not a generalist prompt-jockey, but the person who knows how to apply agents to *your* field's real problems.
2. **Learn to supervise, not just produce** — practice directing, checking, and correcting AI output. That's the new core skill.
3. **Build a portfolio of leverage** — show you can do the work of several people by orchestrating AI well.
4. **Stay a learner** — the field reinvents itself every ~18 months; the meta-skill is fast, fearless re-learning.
5. **Lean into the human edge** — judgment, trust, ethics, relationships, and taste don't automate.
slides.speakerNotes: Read the room — some will be anxious. Lead with empowerment, not fear: 'The goal isn't to compete with AI; it's to become the person who wields it.' The 'replaces tasks, not people' framing defuses panic and is honest. Ask each person to name one task in their job they'd happily hand to an agent and one part that's irreducibly human.
slides.marketConnection: Mid-2026 hiring increasingly rewards 'domain expert + AI orchestrator' profiles over both pure technologists and AI-averse specialists; AI fluency has become a baseline professional expectation.

slides.title: What This Means for Business
slides.body:
## For the leaders in the room

The career logic scales up to the enterprise. The 2026 lesson from Day 19 holds: the winners aren't those with the flashiest pilots — they're those who *operationalize* AI on the right tasks with discipline.

## The strategic playbook

- **Start from the task, not the technology.** Find work with the right shape — high-volume, verifiable, bounded, human-checkpoint-friendly — and apply AI there first. Resist 'AI for AI's sake.'
- **Measure ruthlessly.** If you can't tell whether it's working, you're gambling. Build evaluation in from day one.
- **Redesign workflows, don't just bolt AI on.** The biggest gains come from rethinking the process around agents, not pasting a chatbot onto the old one.
- **Invest in the boring infrastructure.** Guardrails, observability, MCP integrations, model routing — the unglamorous plumbing is where reliable value lives.
- **Treat models as a fleet.** Don't marry one vendor; build a thin abstraction and swap as price/capability move.
- **Manage the risk seriously.** Security (prompt injection!), data governance, compliance, and human oversight are not optional in production.

## The competitive reality

```
   LAGGARDS              EXPERIMENTERS          OPERATORS
   'too risky'           endless pilots          AI in production,
   doing nothing    ->   no measured value  ->   measured, scaling,
                                                  workflow-redesigned
```

The gap between 'experimenting' and 'operating' is the defining competitive divide of mid-2026. Most companies are stuck in the middle column. The advice you give could move yours to the right.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the executive payoff slide. Hammer 'start from the task, not the technology' — it's the most common, most expensive mistake. The three-column maturity diagram is a mirror: ask leaders which column they're honestly in. Most say 'experimenters,' which opens the conversation about what it takes to become 'operators.'
slides.marketConnection: The pilot-to-production gap is the single most-discussed enterprise AI theme of mid-2026; the firms crossing it are pulling measurably ahead of those running perpetual experiments.

slides.title: Now You Teach: The Mindset Shift
slides.body:
## The final transformation

Everything until now made you a *master* of this material. The rest of today makes you a *teacher* of it — a different, harder, more rewarding skill. Knowing something and helping someone else know it are not the same.

## The teacher's core beliefs

- **You don't have to know everything.** This field moves too fast for anyone to know it all. Your job isn't omniscience — it's to give learners a sturdy *map* and the confidence to keep exploring. 'I don't know, let's find out' is a power move, not a weakness.
- **Stories beat specs.** People forget bullet points; they remember the campfire story of attention, the USB-C analogy for MCP, the 'employee vs. consultant' agent. Teach in metaphors and narratives — that's how this course was built and why it stuck.
- **Understanding > coverage.** Better to deeply land five ideas than to skim fifty. If learners leave with the *arc* (Day 20, Slide 2) and the *principles* (Slide 3), they can rebuild the details themselves.
- **Meet them where they are.** A room of executives needs different emphasis than a room of engineers. Same story, different altitude.
- **Energy is contagious.** Your genuine excitement about this material is the most important teaching tool you have. If you're fascinated, they will be too.

## The shift in one line

Stop asking 'do I know this?' Start asking 'how do I make *them* feel they know this?' That reframing is the whole craft.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the emotional pivot of the day — the learner becomes the teacher. Share your own teaching failure or nerves to humanize it. The 'I don't know, let's find out' permission slip is liberating for new teachers terrified of being asked something they can't answer. Ask: 'What's the one analogy from these 20 days that finally made something click for YOU?' — those are the analogies they should reuse.
slides.marketConnection: As of mid-2026, demand for people who can train others on AI vastly outstrips supply — the ability to teach this is itself a scarce, monetizable skill.

slides.title: How to Teach It Well: Pacing and Demos
slides.body:
## The practical mechanics

Great content fails with bad delivery. Here's how to run this course live, room after room.

### Pacing
- **One big idea per segment.** Don't cram. Each day here has a spine — teach the spine, let details hang off it.
- **Rhythm: tell, show, ask.** State the concept (tell), demonstrate or illustrate it (show), then engage the room with a question (ask). Repeat. Never lecture for more than ~10 minutes without interaction.
- **Budget for questions.** Plan ~70% content, 30% discussion. The discussion is where learning consolidates, not the slides.
- **Read the energy.** After lunch, do a demo or activity, not a dense theory slide. Pacing is emotional, not just temporal.

### Demos — your secret weapon
Nothing convinces like *seeing it work*. Build a small kit of reliable live demos:
- A model **reasoning** through a hard problem step by step.
- A model **calling a tool** via MCP and narrating the result (Day 19, Slide 6).
- A **coding agent** fixing a failing test and opening a PR.
- A **computer-use** agent operating a real app (sandboxed!).

**Demo rules:** always have a recorded backup (live demos fail at the worst moment); explain what they're about to see *before* you run it; narrate while it runs; debrief what just happened after. A demo without narration is just a screen; a narrated demo is a revelation.

### A simple session template
```
  HOOK (story/question)            5%
  TELL (the core idea)            25%
  SHOW (demo / diagram / example) 25%
  ASK  (discussion / exercise)    30%
  RECAP + bridge to next          15%
```
slides.speakerNotes: Stress the 'recorded backup' rule from hard-won experience — live AI demos WILL fail eventually, and a calm fallback separates pros from amateurs. Have the class practice the tell-show-ask rhythm on a single concept (e.g., MCP) in pairs. The session template is something they can literally print and follow tomorrow.
slides.marketConnection: Live, working demos are especially persuasive in mid-2026 because audiences have heard endless AI claims — seeing an agent actually do the work cuts through the skepticism instantly.

slides.title: How to Teach It Well: Questions and Misconceptions
slides.body:
## Handling questions like a pro

- **Welcome the hard ones.** A tough question is a gift — it means someone is engaged. Never bluff. 'Great question — I'm not certain, here's how I'd find out' builds more trust than a confident wrong answer.
- **Redirect to the room.** 'Does anyone else have a view on that?' turns a Q&A into a discussion and surfaces peer expertise.
- **Bridge back to principles.** Most detailed questions can be answered by returning to the five durable truths (Slide 3) or the arc (Slide 2). Anchor specifics to fundamentals.
- **Park the rabbit holes.** Use a 'parking lot' for off-track but interesting tangents so one person doesn't hijack the room.

## The misconceptions you MUST correct

These come up in every room. Be ready with the crisp correction.

| Misconception | The correction |
|---|---|
| 'AI actually understands like a human.' | It's a powerful pattern-modeler; whether that's 'understanding' is open. Don't overclaim either way. |
| 'It's just autocomplete, nothing to see.' | Equally wrong — emergence and reasoning are real and surprising. Both extremes are lazy. |
| 'The model just *knows* current facts.' | It knows its training data; for fresh/true facts it needs RAG or tools. Hallucination is structural, not a bug to be fully patched. |
| 'Bigger is always better.' | Routing, efficiency, and the *right* model for the task often beat raw size. |
| 'Agents are reliable and autonomous now.' | Long-horizon reliability is the open problem; production agents need guardrails and human checkpoints. |
| 'AI will replace everyone tomorrow.' | It replaces *tasks*; the workflow and human-supervision reality is more gradual and more nuanced. |
| 'Prompting is the whole skill.' | Prompting matters, but systems, evaluation, tools, and judgment matter more for real value. |

## The meta-rule

The truth almost always lives *between* the hype and the dismissal. Your highest service as a teacher is to keep pulling learners back to that honest middle.
slides.speakerNotes: The misconceptions table is arguably the most practically useful thing in the whole capstone — these EXACT myths will surface in every session. Have learners role-play: one plays a skeptic stating a misconception, the other corrects it warmly. The 'truth lives between hype and dismissal' line should be your recurring refrain as a teacher.
slides.marketConnection: In mid-2026's noisy AI discourse, a teacher who can calmly correct both the hypesters and the dismissers is uniquely valuable — that balanced credibility is rare and sought-after.

slides.title: Your Capstone Challenge
slides.body:
## Prove it to yourself

You don't truly own this knowledge until you've used it and taught it. Your capstone has two parts — one of *doing*, one of *teaching*.

### Part A — Build or design something real
Choose one:
1. **Build a small agent.** Wire a model to one or two tools (via MCP if you can), give it a bounded task from your own work, add a simple guardrail, and run it. Document what worked and what broke.
2. **Design an AI solution.** For a real problem in your job/company, produce a one-page design: the task and why its shape fits, the model/routing choice, the tools/MCP, the orchestration, the guardrails, and how you'd measure ROI.
3. **Write the explainer.** Produce a piece (article, deck, or short video) that explains one course concept to a complete beginner using a fresh analogy of your own.

### Part B — Teach a 10-minute lesson
Pick *one* concept from the 20 days. Prepare and deliver a 10-minute lesson on it using the tell–show–ask rhythm, with one analogy and one question for the audience. Teach it to a colleague, a friend, or this group.

## The bar to clear

```
  Can you...
  [ ] tell the 20-day arc as one story, from memory?
  [ ] explain MCP, agents, and RAG to a non-technical person?
  [ ] name the open problems honestly?
  [ ] decide if a given task is a good fit for an agent?
  [ ] correct the top 3 misconceptions on the spot?
  [ ] teach one concept for 10 minutes and make it land?
```

Clear that checklist and you are, without exaggeration, in the top tier of AI-literate professionals as of mid-2026 — and ready to multiply that literacy in others.
slides.speakerNotes: Give people a real deadline and a real audience for Part B — teaching is only learned by doing it. If time allows, have two or three volunteers deliver their 10-minute lesson live and give warm, specific feedback. The checklist is their graduation rubric; encourage them to keep it and self-assess in a month.
slides.marketConnection: A portfolio piece (a working agent, a credible design doc, or a polished explainer) plus the proven ability to teach is, as of mid-2026, a powerful differentiator in any AI-adjacent role or business.

slides.title: The Torch — A Closing Charge
slides.body:
## Eight words, twenty days, one journey

We began with eight words: *attention is all you need.* We end with you, able to explain, apply, and teach the most consequential technology of our era. That is not a small thing. That is a genuine transformation, and you earned it day by day.

## What you carry out of this room

- **A story** — the arc from attention to agents, ready to tell.
- **A map** — of what's solved, what's in progress, and what's gloriously unknown.
- **A compass** — principles to navigate by when the next reinvention arrives (and it will, within months).
- **A torch** — the ability to light the same understanding in others.

## The charge

This field will keep changing faster than any of us can fully track. That is not a reason for fear; it is the most exciting invitation of our lifetimes. The people who will shape what comes next aren't the ones who memorized today's model names — they're the ones who understand the *principles*, stay *curious*, act with *judgment and ethics*, and lift others as they climb.

That's you now.

So here is the charge, plainly: **Go use it. Go build with it. And above all, go teach it.** Every person you bring up this mountain makes the whole field wiser, safer, and more human. The revolution doesn't need more spectators. It needs more guides.

The torch is yours. Carry it well — and pass it on.

> *'The best way to learn is to teach. The best way to master is to lead someone else to mastery. You are ready for both.'*
slides.speakerNotes: This is the emotional crescendo — slow down, lower your voice, mean every word. End on the torch metaphor and let silence sit for a beat before applause. If it fits the group, invite each person to say one word for how they feel finishing the course — a powerful, bonding closer. Then congratulate them, genuinely. They climbed a real mountain.
slides.marketConnection: As of mid-2026, the differentiator is no longer access to AI — everyone has that — but the depth to understand it and the generosity to teach it; that is the lasting edge these graduates now hold.

keyTakeaways: The entire course is one story: from attention to Transformers to scale to alignment to multimodality, RAG, and efficiency, and finally to tool-using, acting agents — eight years in a straight line.
Mastery includes honesty about the open problems: reasoning limits, alignment, cost/energy, and the data wall remain genuinely unsolved as of mid-2026.
Hold the AGI and future-direction debates (world models, embodied AI) with informed humility — the truth lives between the hype and the dismissal.
Careers and businesses win by moving up the stack: from doing tasks to directing and verifying AI, and from endless pilots to measured production on well-shaped tasks.
Teaching is a distinct craft: lead with stories and analogies, run the tell-show-ask rhythm with reliable demos, welcome hard questions, and correct the recurring misconceptions.
You leave with a story to tell, a map of the unknown, principles to navigate by, and a torch to pass on — you are now a guide, not a spectator.
marketSnapshot2026: As of June 2026, AI literacy has become a baseline professional expectation, but genuine understanding — and the ability to teach it — remains scarce and highly valued. The market is defined by a tightly clustered frontier of capable models, the agent/MCP/computer-use revolution moving from promise to production, and a stark divide between organizations experimenting endlessly and those operating AI with measured ROI and serious guardrails. The biggest debates (AGI timelines, reasoning's true nature, alignment, energy and the data wall) are unresolved and shape capital and policy. In this landscape, the professionals who thrive pair deep domain expertise with AI fluency, the judgment to supervise rather than merely produce, and — increasingly — the rare ability to bring others up to speed. The person who finishes this course is positioned not just to use the AI revolution, but to lead and teach within it.
homework: Complete the two-part capstone. Part A: build a small bounded agent (model + a tool or two via MCP + one guardrail) OR write a one-page AI solution design for a real problem in your work OR create a beginner explainer of one course concept using your own fresh analogy. Part B: prepare and deliver a 10-minute lesson on one concept from the 20 days using the tell-show-ask rhythm, with one analogy and one audience question. Then self-assess against the capstone checklist (Day 20, Slide 12) and schedule your first real teaching session with a colleague, team, or community within the next 30 days. Finally, write a short note to your future self: where do you think AI — and you — will be one year from now?
nextDayTeaser: There is no Day 21 — and that's the point. You've completed the journey: twenty days from 'attention is all you need' to standing ready to teach this revolution to others. You came in a learner and you leave a guide. The frontier will keep moving, and now you have the story, the map, the principles, and the torch to move with it — and to bring others along. Congratulations. Class, you are dismissed. Go light some fires.
